title: Fixed income — lending, not saving
subtitle: Bonds are loans. When you buy one, you are a creditor. Understanding this reframes what you expect, what you earn, and what can go wrong.

body.content: A bond is a loan. When a government or company issues a bond, they are borrowing money from investors and promising to return it with interest. As a bond investor, you are a lender — not an owner. That distinction changes your return profile entirely: you get a fixed income stream and your principal back, but you do not participate in upside if the borrower prospers.

body.label: Bond mechanics
body.content: A bond has three key parameters: face value (the principal amount returned at maturity), coupon (the periodic interest payment expressed as a percentage of face value), and maturity (when the principal is repaid). The yield is the actual return you earn given the price you paid — which may differ from the coupon if you bought the bond at a premium or discount.

The most important and least intuitive fact about bonds: their price moves inversely to interest rates. When rates rise, existing bond prices fall. When rates fall, existing bond prices rise. This is arithmetic: if you hold a bond paying 3% and new bonds are issued at 5%, your 3% bond is worth less to any potential buyer. The market adjusts price to equalize yields.
The role of fixed income in a portfolio

Bonds serve three purposes: income generation, capital preservation, and portfolio ballast. In environments where equities fall sharply, high-quality government bonds often rise — providing a counterweight. This negative correlation with equities in stress environments is what makes fixed income a portfolio stabilizer, not just a return source.

Fixed income is not a synonym for safe. It carries credit risk, interest rate risk, and inflation risk. What it provides is predictable income and, for high-quality government bonds, a reliable counterbalance to equity volatility.
